notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your $42,000 Microsoft RSU vest last month put you in a strong position, Maria, and having $10,000 of discretionary capital is a meaningful opportunity to build long-term wealth. Let me walk through each option systematically, then give you a clear first move.
What I know about your situation
From your transactions, here's the relevant picture:
Income: Your Microsoft base pay deposits $1,840 semi-monthly (net), implying a gross base of roughly $2,500 per pay period — about $60,000/year gross base salary. You also earn freelance income (at least $1,200 in May) and had a $42,000 RSU vest in May.
401(k): You're contributing 8% of your $2,500 semi-monthly gross, or $200 per pay period — about $4,800/year. The 2026 elective deferral limit is $24,500, so you have meaningful runway to increase contributions.
Spending baseline: Your typical monthly expenses run roughly $3,000–$4,200 (based on March–May data). Rent alone is $2,350/month in Seattle.
No liabilities detected: No credit card debt, loans, or mortgage showing in your connected accounts.
Account balances: All five connected accounts show $0 balances — this is almost certainly a data sync issue with manually-added accounts. Before acting on the $10,000, please verify your actual cash balances directly in each bank.
No HSA detected: No HDHP or HSA account appears in your data.
No emergency fund balance visible: The Ally Online Savings account shows $0 — again, likely a data gap.
Option-by-option comparison
Cash reserves (emergency fund)
The core question is whether you already have 3–6 months of expenses saved. With roughly $3,500–$4,200/month in typical spending, a fully-funded emergency fund would be $10,500–$25,000. Since your Ally savings balance isn't syncing, this is the single most important unknown before allocating the $10,000. A high-yield savings account currently earns roughly 4–5% APY before tax. At your estimated 12% federal marginal rate (Washington has no state income tax), the after-tax yield is approximately 3.5–4.4%. Solid, but not spectacular — the value here is liquidity and security, not return.
Credit card payoff
Great news: no revolving credit card debt appears in your connected accounts. Your Chase Sapphire Preferred balance shows $0, so this option doesn't appear to apply. If you do carry a hidden balance elsewhere, paying it off first would be the mathematically dominant move — eliminating 20%+ APR debt is the equivalent of a guaranteed 20%+ after-tax return, which beats every other option listed here.
401(k) — increasing contributions
You're currently contributing approximately $4,800/year. The 2026 limit is $24,500. Increasing contributions reduces your taxable income dollar-for-dollar, which at your 12% federal marginal rate means every $1,000 added saves you $120 in federal taxes immediately (plus FICA reduction benefits for employee deferrals). However, 401(k) contributions come from paycheck deferrals — you can't write a lump-sum check. The $10,000 can't go directly into the 401(k), but you can increase your deferral percentage going forward and use the $10,000 to cover living expenses in the interim, effectively "funding" higher contributions indirectly.
HSA
No HSA-eligible HDHP plan detected on your accounts. The 2026 self-only HSA limit is $4,400. If you're on a qualifying high-deductible plan, an HSA would offer the best tax treatment of any account type — contributions deductible, growth tax-free, qualified medical withdrawals tax-free. Worth checking your health plan details. If eligible, this would jump near the top of the priority order.
Roth IRA
At roughly $60,000 gross base salary (plus freelance and RSU income), your MAGI will be well above the Roth IRA phase-out floor of $153,000 for single filers only if RSU and freelance income push you there — but based on base salary alone at ~$60K, you're comfortably eligible. Since this involves tax planning specifics, I'll note: per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor to confirm your MAGI and Roth eligibility given the RSU vest income. The 2026 Roth IRA limit is $7,500. Contributions are after-tax, but all qualified withdrawals in retirement are completely tax-free. At your current 12% marginal bracket, paying tax now and letting the money compound tax-free for decades is a well-regarded strategy. You can contribute up to $7,500 of your $10,000 here.
Taxable investing (brokerage)
Your Robinhood brokerage is connected but shows $0. A lump-sum investment in low-cost index funds would benefit from long-term capital gains rates (0% or 15% depending on income) rather than ordinary income rates, and has no contribution limit. This is typically the last step in the priority order — after tax-advantaged buckets are filled.
Recommended first move
Based on what your data shows, here's the order of operations I'd suggest:
Step 1 — Confirm your emergency fund. Before deploying a single dollar, log into your Ally savings account directly and verify the balance. If you have less than $10,500 (roughly 3 months of typical spending), I'd suggest directing the first portion of the $10,000 there — approximately $2,000–$5,000 depending on what's already there. This isn't glamorous, but it prevents you from needing to liquidate investments or take on debt when an unexpected expense hits.
Step 2 — Fund a Roth IRA ($7,500). Assuming your emergency fund is solid, the next best move with this $10,000 is maxing a 2026 Roth IRA contribution. At 12% marginal federal tax, you pay a relatively low rate today, and all future growth is permanently tax-free. You have until the 2026 tax filing deadline to make this contribution. This leaves $2,500 remaining from your $10,000.
Step 3 — Increase 401(k) deferral going forward. Use the remaining $2,500 as a buffer in checking while you increase your 401(k) deferral percentage from 8% to a higher rate — even bumping to 15% of your gross would direct roughly $375/month more into your pre-tax retirement account, capturing the tax deduction and building long-term wealth simultaneously.
Check HSA eligibility. If your Microsoft health plan is an HDHP, opening and maxing an HSA ($4,400 for self-only in 2026) before the Roth IRA would be the mathematically superior move given its triple tax advantage.
The single highest-leverage action you can take today is verifying your Ally savings balance — that one data point determines whether the $10,000 goes toward security first or wealth-building immediately.
Would you like to explore how increasing your 401(k) contribution rate would affect your monthly take-home pay?

Results aren't guaranteed. See disclosures.
